query: Ursula von der Leyen April 2025 EU defence investment plan official name budget amount

mako.raw:
{"query":"Ursula von der Leyen April 2025 EU defence investment plan official name budget amount","model":"mako","response_time_ms":1528,"verdict":null,"extra_info":null,"results":[{"url":"https://www.bundesfinanzministerium.de/Content/EN/Pressemitteilungen/2025/2025-07-30-government-draft-2026-federal-budget.html","title":"Federal Ministry of Finance - German government intensifies its investment drive: 2026 federal budget and fiscal plan to 2029 adopted","content":"2026 federal budget and fiscal plan to 2029 adopted
Less than three months after taking office, the German government has already adopted two budgets, for 2025 and 2026.
Investment in 2026 will increase by over €10 billion on the year to €126.7 billion.
To make Germany fit for the future, the government is placing a particular priority on investments in transport infrastructure, education and childcare, housing construction, hospitals, digital technology, climate action, and internal and external security.
The principle of additionality will be applied in 2026 and throughout the entire fiscal planning period. €48.9 billion of the total investment amount will be drawn from the Special Fund for Infrastructure and Climate Neutrality, and €21.7 billion will be drawn from the Climate and Transformation Fund.
Investment in transport infrastructure will total €166 billion in the four years up to 2029.
For example, for the second consecutive year, the Federation will allocate nearly €2 billion in additional VAT revenue to the Länder in 2026 in order to support the process of improving child daycare quality nationwide.
The number of soldiers in the German armed forces is expected to increase by up to 10,000 in 2026.
In addition to providing for massive increases in investment, the 2026 federal budget contains a wide range of relief measures for citizens."},{"url":"https://epthinktank.eu/2026/03/09/eu-member-states-defence-budgets-2/","title":"EU Member States’ defence budgets | Epthinktank | European Parliament","content":"Russia’s war on Ukraine has been a wake-up call for many EU Member States in terms of defence budgets.
The 23 EU Member States that are also NATO members have long been guided by NATO’s 2 % of GDP defence spending commitment formalised at the 2014 Wales Summit.
The European Commission notes that, if all Member States had spent 2 % of GDP on defence from 2006 to 2020, this would have amounted to an extra €1.1 trillion for defence spending.
At the March 2022 Versailles Summit, EU leaders agreed to spend ‘more and better’ on defence.
The second von der Leyen Commission (2024-2029) has made defence a key EU priority.
On 4 March 2025, Commission President Ursula von der Leyen presented the ReArm Europe plan/ Readiness 2030.
On 19 March 2025, the Commission presented its white paper for European defence, outlining a strategic plan to close critical capability gaps, strengthen the defence industry and readiness, support Ukraine, and boost innovation and partnerships to ensure the continent’s long-term security.
In 2025, Member States reached an estimated €381 billion (2.1 % of GDP).
All EU NATO Allies now spend more than the 2 % defence expenditure benchmark agreed at the 2014 NATO Summit.
EU defence investment reached €106 billion in 2024 and was set to approach €130 billion in 2025.
The Netherlands has more than doubled its defence budget since 2021, reaching €25.8 billion in 2025, around 2.2 % of GDP.
Over the past two decades, defence spending in Russia and China has more than doubled in real terms; Member States’ expenditure has risen by just over 50 % since 2008."}]}

President Ursula von der LEYEN on the defence package","score":0.7216064,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.euronews.com/my-europe/2025/03/04/von-der-leyen-pitches-800bn-defence-package-ahead-of-eu-leaders-summit","title":"Von der Leyen pitches '€800bn' defence package ahead of EU leaders' summit | Euronews","content":"Ursula von der Leyen on Tuesday proposed a Rearm Europe Plan she said could see member states mobilise up to €800 billion to finance a massive ramp-up in defence spending, hours after Washington suspended all military aid to Ukraine putting pressure on the bloc to increase its own assistance. \"We are in an era of re-armament, and Europe is ready to massively boost its defence spending, both to respond to the short-term urgency to act and to support Ukraine, but also to address the long-term need to take on more responsibility for our own European security,\" the European Commission president said. The five measures she outlined, which she details in a letter to EU leaders ahead of an extraordinary summit on Thursday, include a \"new instrument\" to raise €150 billion on the markets to then provide that money as loans to member states that want to invest in capabilities including missile defence, artillery systems, missiles and ammunition, drones and anti-drone systems or in military mobility.","score":0.70263255,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.dw.com/en/eus-von-der-leyen-proposes-800-billion-defense-plan/a-71819582","title":"EU's von der Leyen proposes €800 billion defense plan","content":"# EU's von der Leyen proposes €800 billion defense plan. The European Commission president says increasing defense spending is necessary in \"an era of rearmament.\" A European summit on defense and Ukraine is planned this week amid tense transatlantic relations. European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen unveiled a major defense spending plan ahead of a meeting with EU leaders later this weekImage: Wiktor Dabkowski/ZUMA Press Wire/IMAGO. European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen on Tuesday presented a plan to bolster Europe's defense industry and increase military capabilities. \"We are in an era of rearmament, and Europe is ready to massively boost its defense spending,\" Von der Leyen said. Von der Leyen had suggested the rearmament plan after emerging from the closed-door meeting on Ukraine at Lancaster House in London over the weekend. Von der Leyen outlined the five-point plan at a critical time for the future of Europe's security. Skip next section More on Politics from Europe. European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen delivers a statement on plans to strengthen the European defence industry and the EU's military capabilities, in Brussels, Belgium March 4, 2025. BRUSSELS, March 4 (Reuters) - The European Commission proposed on Tuesday to borrow up to 150 billion euros ($157.76 billion) to lend to EU governments under a rearmament plan driven by Russia's war in Ukraine and fears that Europe can no longer be sure of U.S. protection. Much of the Commission package focuses on encouraging the EU's member states to spend more on defence and repurposing existing funds rather than providing new EU money.
